title: KL Divergence Between Two Distributions
description:
Implement the Kullback-Leibler divergence between two discrete distributions derived from unnormalized positive weight vectors $p, q \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}^n$. First normalize each vector to sum to 1:
$$\tilde{p}_i = \frac{p_i}{\sum_{j} p_j}, \qquad \tilde{q}_i = \frac{q_i}{\sum_{j} q_j}$$
then compute
$$D_{KL}(\tilde{p} \parallel \tilde{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tilde{p}_i \log\!\left(\frac{\tilde{p}_i}{\tilde{q}_i}\right)$$

Implement `kl_divergence(p, q)` returning a single scalar. Both inputs are strictly positive, so no clamping is needed.

**Shapes**: `p`, `q`: `(5,)` -> returns a scalar.
starter_code:
import numpy as np

def kl_divergence(p, q):

    pass
reference:
import numpy as np

def kl_divergence(p, q):
    p = np.asarray(p, dtype=np.float64)
    q = np.asarray(q, dtype=np.float64)
    p_norm = p / np.sum(p)
    q_norm = q / np.sum(q)
    return np.sum(p_norm * np.log(p_norm / q_norm))
hints: Normalize p and q separately by dividing each by its own sum, so both become valid probability distributions that sum to 1.
The KL divergence is sum(p_norm * log(p_norm / q_norm)) — compute it elementwise on the normalized vectors, then sum.

solutions.name: Normalize to distributions, then sum p*log(p/q)
solutions.explanation: KL divergence requires proper probability distributions, so the raw positive weights are first normalized to sum to 1. The divergence $\sum_i \tilde p_i \log(\tilde p_i/\tilde q_i)$ measures the extra bits needed to encode samples from $\tilde p$ using a code optimized for $\tilde q$; it is 0 iff $\tilde p = \tilde q$ and is non-negative by Gibbs' inequality.
solutions.code:
import numpy as np
def kl_divergence(p, q):
    p = np.asarray(p, dtype=np.float64)
    q = np.asarray(q, dtype=np.float64)
    p_norm = p / np.sum(p)
    q_norm = q / np.sum(q)
    return np.sum(p_norm * np.log(p_norm / q_norm))
